notes: The record exposes 1440 in a created/date field, but that value reflects the medieval work or witness era rather than the separately stated 1992 digital issue date.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1976. U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A states that qualifying works copyrighted in 1964–1977 renew automatically and can receive a 95-year term; absent edition-specific notice and ownership research, protectable 1976 contributions should not be presumed public domain merely because the medieval text is old.
rights.component: Original introduction, critical apparatus, notes, glossary, selection and arrangement, and any sufficiently original editorial readings
notes: If federal copyright was validly secured, a 95-year term measured from 1976 would run through 2071. This is conditional, not a finding that every individual reading or transcription choice is protected.
Open Library/Internet Archive borrowing does not authorize redistribution.

access.accessMethod: Apply in writing with the subject and purpose of research, provide an academic letter of introduction, and book the reading room in advance; confirm item availability during library works.
notes: The public page says all library resources, including medieval manuscripts, are available for study, but also reports temporary collection-access limitations during Wren Library repairs. Availability should be confirmed before travel.
The academic-letter requirement may make access difficult for independent researchers; contact the librarian to ask what alternative evidence of a serious research purpose is acceptable.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item AllitMA. U-M's header says the electronic text was made available through the Oxford Text Archive and identifies Valerie Krishna's 1976 critical edition as its printed source. Krishna's edition represents the poem's sole surviving witness, Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 (the Lincoln Thornton manuscript), fols. 53r–98v. For visual comparison, U-M's encoding declaration cites the 1975 Scolar Press facsimile of the complete Thornton manuscript. A modern METS edition is linked as a separate comparison source, not as an upstream source of this repository file.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Late fourteenth century, probably around 1400; sole surviving copy made c. 1430–1450
catalogSummary.region: Probably the North Midlands of England; sole surviving copy associated with Robert Thornton of East Newton, Yorkshire
catalogSummary.title: The Alliterative Morte Arthure

catalogingSubject.label: The Alliterative Morte Arthure
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-medieval Middle English alliterative poem represented by CME item AllitMA. The work is distinct from Robert Thornton's sole surviving copy, Valerie Krishna's 1976 critical edition, the Oxford Text Archive and University of Michigan digital texts, and this repository's XML artifact.

contentParts.label: The Alliterative Morte Arthure
notes: This summary-level part deliberately does not treat the 80 XML divisions as intrinsic chapters.
contentParts.summary: The complete alliterative narrative from the opening invocation and promise to tell of Arthur's deeds through the Roman campaign, Mordred's usurpation, Arthur's final battle, death, and burial.

assertion.notes: The METS introduction explicitly describes a date around 1400 and North Midlands origin as informed guesses rather than certainties.

notes: This dates the extant copy, not necessarily the poem's composition.

extent.description: The local XML contains one complete poem of 4,346 numbered verse lines, grouped in 232 LG elements and divided into 80 editorial/structural DIV1 ranges. It also records 117 printed-page breaks, 92 manuscript-folio milestones from 53r through 98v, and 40 SUPPLIED elements. The 80 divisions are encoding units rather than authorial chapters.

formStatements.description: Unrhymed alliterative long-line verse; each line is divided rhythmically into two half-lines joined by alliteration.

assertion.notes: The poem treats Arthur's final wars through the sober chronicle-history tradition while retaining romance form and matter.

scope.description: Short embedded devotional or liturgical phrases, including 'In manus' near the close

notes: The U-M interface's publication date c. 1976 is the date of Krishna's source edition, not the date of the medieval work.
The OTA record's date 1440 describes the witness-era text and should not be treated as a securely known composition year.
Rights and access for the 1976 edition, digital encodings, manuscript text, and manuscript photographs are documented separately in the lineage manifest.

next_steps: Review recent linguistic and historical scholarship beyond the general METS dating summary.
Record competing date and dialect proposals as separate assertions if they materially differ.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition be localized or dated more narrowly without converting disputed scholarship into a fact?

next_steps: Consult current authorship scholarship before adding Huchoun or another attributed author.
openQuestions.question: Is any proposed named authorship supported strongly enough to record beyond an open attribution history?

next_steps: Ask Lincoln Cathedral Library whether a complete digital surrogate exists and what reuse terms apply.
Use the 1975 Scolar Press facsimile through a holding library for visual comparison in the interim.
openQuestions.question: Is a complete, openly accessible digital facsimile of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 now available?

assertion.notes: The source presents this localization as probable, not certain.

notes: The manuscript was written by and for Robert Thornton of East Newton; this does not prove that the poem itself was composed there.

scope.description: Association of the sole surviving copy through its scribe and owner

notes: Historical attributions to Huchoun are not treated as established authorship in this record.

scope.description: The 1976 source edition underlying the OTA/U-M transcription

notes: This is witness-level scribal responsibility, not authorship of the work.

scope.description: The sole surviving manuscript copy at Lincoln Cathedral

subjects.label: The fall and death of King Arthur

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English alliterative poem recounting Arthur's refusal of Roman tribute, his continental war against Emperor Lucius, and the catastrophic return to Britain after Mordred's betrayal. Its chronicle-like interest in campaigns, cities, kingship, and bodily warfare culminates in the destruction of the Round Table and Arthur's death.

The poem survives in one known medieval copy, Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, fols. 53r–98v. Robert Thornton copied that witness in the fifteenth century; his scribal identity must not be converted into authorial attribution.

notes: The initial article is conventional in modern cataloging; the manuscript rubric uses the shorter form Morte Arthure.

assertion.notes: Lincoln Cathedral's record transcribes the opening rubric as 'He begynnes Morte Arthure' and the closing as 'Here endes Morte Arthure writen by Robert of Thorntone.'

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Late fourteenth century, probably around 1400; sole surviving copy made c. 1430–1450
work.regionDisplay: Probably the North Midlands of England; sole surviving copy associated with Robert Thornton of East Newton, Yorkshire

subjects.label: The fall and death of King Arthur

work.summary: An anonymous Middle English alliterative poem recounting Arthur's refusal of Roman tribute, his continental war against Emperor Lucius, and the catastrophic return to Britain after Mordred's betrayal. Its chronicle-like interest in campaigns, cities, kingship, and bodily warfare culminates in the destruction of the Round Table and Arthur's death.
